macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna on Shomrim and the Standard of Care for Money

micro_segments.title: Opening Context: Transition from Special Cases to Basic Categories of Shomrim
The Mishna's Cases: Bundling Money, Giving to Minors, and Locking Up
The Standard of Derech Hashomrim and Its Contextual Nature

macro_segments.title: Rav Yitzchak's Ruling: Money Must Be Held in Hand, Not in Back Pocket

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Challenge to the Mishna: What Should Be Done Instead of Bundling and Putting Behind Back?
Rav Yitzchak's Ruling from the Verse: Money Must Be in Your Hand
Connection to Earlier Gemara: People Check Their Pockets Constantly

macro_segments.title: Rav Yitzchak's Aggadic Teachings on Money Management and Blessing

micro_segments.title: Always Have Cash Available for Opportunities
Diversify Your Assets: One Third Land, One Third Commerce, One Third Liquid
Blessing Is Found Only in What Is Hidden from the Eye
Sources and Interpretations of the Hidden Blessing Principle
Application to Measuring Grain: When to Say Blessings and the Problem of Measuring

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's Ruling: Money Requires the Highest Level of Care—Burial in the Ground

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's Statement: Money Can Only Be Protected by Burying It in the Ground
Rava's Exception: Before Shabbat, the Rabbis Did Not Demand Burial
The Principle That Standard of Care Is Shaped by Societal Norms, Not Just Risk Prevention
Progressive Thieves and the Escalating Requirements: Ground, Walls, Hidden Bricks
How Deep Must Money Be Buried? Comparison to Chametz and the Answer of One Tefach

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Question: When Money Is Buried and Still Stolen, Is That an Oness for Shomer Sachar?

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's Doubt: If Money Is Properly Buried and Then Stolen, Is the Shomer Sachar Exempt?
Tosafot's Investigation: Conflicting Sources on Shomer Sachar's Liability for Gneiva Be'Ones
Tosafot's Resolution: Armed Robbers (Lisim Mezuyan) as Clear Oness vs. Other Gneivot

macro_segments.title: Case of the Hunting Hut: When Negligence in One Area (Fire) Leads to Loss in Another (Theft)

micro_segments.title: The Case: Money Placed in a Duck Blind Out in the Field, Protected from Thieves But Not Fire
Rav Yosef's Ruling: Negligence at the Start, Oness at the End—Still Liable
Dispute: Some Say the Opposite—Negligence in One Area, Protection in Another = Exempt
Final Ruling: Negligence at Start, Oness at End = Liable

macro_segments.title: Case of the Forgotten Money: Not Knowing Where Money Is Placed Constitutes Negligence

micro_segments.title: The Case: Shomer Says He Forgot Where He Buried the Money
Rava's Ruling: Not Knowing Location = Negligence, Must Pay

macro_segments.title: Case of the Son Who Gave Money to His Mother: Implicit Acceptance and Reasonable Assumptions

micro_segments.title: The Case: Money Given to Mother in a Box Without Explaining It Was Entrusted Money
Rava's Analysis: Son Can Argue Mother Thought It Was His Own Money So Would Be Extra Careful
The Oath Solution: Both Son and Mother Swear to Facts; Neither Is Deemed Negligent
Tosafot's Concern: Even If Son Is Allowed to Give to Mother, Isn't He Still Ultimately Responsible?

macro_segments.title: Case of the Orphan's Overseer and the Toothless Cow: Duty of Care and De Facto Shomer Relationships

micro_segments.title: The Case: Overseer Buys Cow for Orphans, Gives to Cowherd, Cow Dies from Not Eating
The Question: Who Is Liable—the Overseer for Not Checking, or the Cowherd for Not Noticing?
Gemara's Response: Cowherd Is a Shomer Sachar and Must Pay Attention to Eating Habits
Complication: Mekach Taus Reversal Means Original Owner Refunds the Orphans—Who Now Owns the Cow?
The Middleman Situation: Owner Requires Cowherd to Have Informed Him So He Could Retrieve It
Resolution: Owner Swears Ignorance, Cowherd Pays Meat Value (Not Full Cow Value)
Tosafot's Deep Point: De Facto Relationships—Cowherd Liable to Owner Even Without Contract

macro_segments.title: Case of the Mixed Hops: Benefit Versus Negligence in Unwitting Use of Another's Property

micro_segments.title: The Case: Shomer Tells Employee to Take Hops from His Pile, Employee Takes from Wrong Pile
Argument Structure: Who Is Negligent—the Shomer for Not Being Clear or the Employee?
Gemara's Shortcut: If the Hops Benefited the Shomer's Beer, Pay Back Regardless of Negligence
Variation: If the Hops Damaged the Beer (Turned It to Vinegar), No Obligation to Pay Back
Rav Ashi's Further Variation: Inferior Hops—Muddy the Benefit Question
Unresolved Tension: Gemara Leaves Unclear Whether Negligence Matters or Only Benefit

topical_tags.term: derech hashomrim (standard of care as defined by convention)
blessing hidden from eye (bracha samui min ha'ayin)
shomer sachar (paid guardian) and theft liability
tchilato bif'shiya vesofo b'ones (negligence at start, oness at end)
shomer she-masar l'shomer (guardian giving to another guardian)
mekach taus (mistaken sale; defective goods return)
lo yadana (I didn't know/I forgot where)
kol hamaphkid al da'as ishto u-vanav (whoever entrusts assumes you may give to wife/children)
